title: Emerging Innovative Border Technologies Act
plain_summary: This law requires the Department of Homeland Security to make a plan within six months to find and use new technologies that will help keep our borders safe. The plan must include special teams that test new technology like artificial intelligence and advanced sensors, and explain how they work with other government agencies and private companies. The law allows Customs and Border Protection to create these Innovation Teams to research and adapt new commercial technologies for border security. The agency must also send yearly reports to Congress about what these teams are doing and how well the new technologies are working.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Emerging Innovative Border Technologies Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Innovative and Emerging Border Technology Plan
sections.text: Within one hundred eighty days after this law is passed, the Secretary of Homeland Security must create a detailed plan. The Secretary will work with the head of Customs and Border Protection (the government agency that protects our borders) and the head of the Science and Technology office at Homeland Security. They must send this plan to two important committees in Congress. The plan must explain how to find, combine, and use new and advanced technologies to make border security better or fix problems with current border security. These technologies might include artificial intelligence (computer systems that can think and learn like humans), machine learning (computers that get better at tasks by practicing), automation (machines that work by themselves), fiber optic sensing (using light through cables to detect things), nanotechnology (working with extremely tiny materials), special types of radar, computer modeling, special sensors that use light and lasers, and systems that can see and identify things. The plan must include twelve specific types of information. First, it must explain how Customs and Border Protection uses special Innovation Teams and other methods to find and use new technologies. Second, it must show what these efforts have accomplished so far. Third, it must describe who is on each Innovation Team and how they work with other offices and partners. Fourth, it must identify technologies that other government agencies use that could help with border security. Fifth, it must analyze what legal authority Customs and Border Protection has to buy new technologies and whether they need more authority. Sixth, it must explain how existing small technology programs can become full permanent programs. Seventh, it must describe each planned security technology program including what it hopes to achieve and when. Eighth, it must assess how security technologies might affect the privacy and safety of people living near the border. Ninth, it must identify old border technology programs that could be replaced with newer ones and estimate the costs. Tenth, it must explain how Customs and Border Protection works with the Science and Technology office to research new technologies, find technologies being developed by private companies that might help with border security, encourage private companies to develop helpful technologies, and find ways to work better with private companies, small businesses, universities, and government labs. Eleventh, it must explain how to measure whether efforts to find and use new technologies are working well. Twelfth, it must identify recent improvements in several types of technology including aircraft sensors and communication systems, unmanned aircraft and ways to stop enemy drones, surveillance technology like mobile vehicles and cameras and radar and towers and sensors and airborne equipment, inspection technology that can see inside containers without using X-rays, technology to find underground tunnels, and communication equipment like radios and broadband internet and small satellites. The plan can also include any other information the Secretary thinks is important. The head of Customs and Border Protection can create one or more Innovation Teams. These teams will research and adapt new commercial technologies that could help with border security and urgent mission needs. The teams will also study what might happen, including bad outcomes, when new technologies are used for border security problems that have not been fully studied yet. Each Innovation Team must create specific operating procedures that clearly explain everyone's roles and responsibilities within the team and with other government and non-government partners. The procedures must also include rules for quickly moving successful technologies into permanent programs. Each team must also create planning and strategic goals that include projected costs, time frames, ways to measure success, and specific targets for how fast technologies move into permanent programs. Within one hundred eighty days after this law passes and then every year after that, the head of Customs and Border Protection must send information about Innovation Team activities to the same two Congressional committees. This information must include copies of the operating procedures and strategic goals, descriptions of technologies each team tested in the previous year including which ones worked well and what security problems they solved, and updates on efforts to move successful technologies into permanent programs.
